critic_raw:
This is a well-constructed experimental design. I'll work through the genuine methodological concerns I can identify, organized by root cause.

**1. Label construction: the backdated proxy introduces asymmetric leakage risk**

The claim is that anchoring positive labels to 'earliest verified compromise onset when available and otherwise to a conservative backdated proxy from investigation open time' produces clean, future-leakage-free labels.

The failure mechanism is subtle but real. When verified onset timestamps are unavailable (which is likely the majority of cases — investigators rarely have forensic precision on onset), the proxy is derived from investigation open time minus some conservative offset. That offset is presumably chosen by domain experts or heuristically. If the offset is calibrated on the same 18-month corpus — even informally — it encodes retrospective knowledge about how long compromises typically precede detection. More concretely: the decision of *which* user-days fall inside the pre-investigation detection window vs. outside it is partially determined by a parameter whose calibration is not clearly isolated from the evaluation data. If the backdating offset is too aggressive (long), user-days that were behaviorally clean get labeled positive, hurting signal quality. If it is too conservative (short), genuine early-compromise behavior gets labeled negative or excluded, and the model never learns the early signals. Neither error is detectable from PR-AUC alone on the test set.

Evidence that would resolve this: (a) report what fraction of positive labels rely on the proxy vs. verified onset; (b) run a sensitivity analysis varying the backdating offset by ±3 and ±7 days and report how PR-AUC on the test set changes — if it moves materially, the label construction is a free parameter being implicitly tuned; (c) compare the feature distributions of proxy-labeled vs. onset-labeled positives to check for systematic differences.

**2. Graph construction: node/edge formation may encode temporal future information not available at scoring time**

The claim is that all features are computed using only information available up to the scoring timestamp.

For tabular features, this is straightforward to enforce — each row is a user-day aggregate. For the heterogeneous graph, it is substantially harder. Graph edges connect users to devices, IPs, mailboxes, and message entities. An edge between user A and IP X at scoring time t means IP X appeared in user A's logs before t. But IP X may also have edges to user B, who was confirmed compromised *before* t. If user B's compromise is known to the graph at construction time (i.e., user B's node carries an attribute derived from their confirmed-ATO label, or their behavioral aggregates shift in a way that reflects post-compromise remediation actions that occurred before t but after B's investigation closed), then scoring user A at time t incorporates a signal that is only available because B's investigation resolved. This is not leakage from the future relative to A's scoring timestamp, but it is leakage from the ground-truth label of a neighboring node. The design says analyst workflow fields and post-investigation annotations are excluded, but behavioral aggregates of confirmed-ATO users after their remediation (e.g., password reset, MFA re-enrollment, device wipe events) would appear in raw telemetry and flow into the graph as edge/node attributes. A graph model could learn 'user connected to recently-remediated account' as a proxy for 'user connected to confirmed ATO,' which is label leakage through the graph topology.

Evidence that would resolve this: (a) specify explicitly whether node attributes for neighboring users are frozen at the neighbor's own scoring timestamp or at the focal user's scoring timestamp — these differ whenever t_neighbor < t_focal; (b) run the ablation of removing all cross-user relational edges (keeping only user-device and user-IP edges) to test whether the gain disappears, which would implicate this channel; (c) check whether the graph model's precision at top 0.5% is disproportionately concentrated on user-days that share graph neighbors with recently-closed ATO investigations.

**3. Class imbalance handling: class-weighted loss for GNN but implicit weighting for GBDT may not be equivalent**

The claim is that 'both receive comparable tuning effort' and the comparison 'tests representational advantage rather than optimization budget.'

GBDT with class-weighted loss (via scale_pos_weight or sample_weight) and GNN with class-weighted loss are not equivalent operations even if the weight ratio is identical, because GBDT's tree-splitting criterion responds to sample weights differently than a neural loss function does. More importantly, the tuning space for class weighting is itself a hyperparameter. If the GNN's class weight is tuned on the validation set (as part of 'comparable tuning effort') but the GBDT's is set by the imbalance ratio or tuned with a different granularity, the comparison is not clean. PR-AUC is threshold-free, so this matters less for the primary metric, but precision at top 0.5% is sensitive to calibration, and Brier score is directly affected by the implicit probability scale. A misconfigured class weight in GBDT can produce well-ranked scores (good AUROC/PR-AUC) but poorly calibrated probabilities (bad Brier), making the GNN appear better-calibrated without any representational advantage.

Evidence that would resolve this: document the class weight search grid for both models and whether it was tuned jointly or identically; report calibration curves (not just Brier scalar) for both models; confirm that Platt scaling or isotonic regression is applied identically post-hoc if calibration is a reporting goal.

**4. The graph pipeline's structural advantage is confounded with its inductive capacity on unseen nodes**

The claim is that the experiment establishes 'whether the graph formulation improves forward-looking detection performance under realistic deployment constraints.'

In a strictly chronological split, the test graph contains nodes (users, devices, IPs) that did not exist in the training graph. The GNN must handle these inductively. The design uses two message-passing layers with recency-weighted edges, which implies a specific inductive strategy (aggregating neighbor features), but new devices and new IPs in the test period have no training-time neighbors. The GNN's performance on these nodes degrades in a way that is structurally different from how GBDT degrades — GBDT simply uses the tabular aggregate features without graph context, which is exactly its normal operating mode. So on new-device-heavy test cases, the GNN is handicapped in a way that is an artifact of the train/test split rather than a property of the deployment scenario. The subgroup analysis by 'unseen-device prevalence' partially addresses this, but only if it is used to identify whether the GNN's overall performance is being dragged down by inductive failure rather than being reported as a general finding.

Evidence that would resolve this: report GNN performance separately on (a) user-days where all neighboring nodes appeared in training, (b) user-days with at least one unseen neighbor. If (a) >> (b) for GNN but the gap is smaller for GBDT, the comparison conflates representational advantage with inductive robustness, and the deployment claim requires qualification.

**Summary of verdict:**

Issues 1 and 2 are genuine methodological flaws that could produce misleading conclusions — specifically, label construction sensitivity and graph-topology label leakage. Issues 3 and 4 are real but more tractable design gaps that the described ablations and subgroup analyses partially address. The design is sophisticated and clearly thought-through, but issues 1 and 2 are not resolved by the described protocol and could cause the experiment to report a spurious advantage (or disadvantage) for the graph model.
all_issues_raised: The backdated proxy label anchor is an implicit free parameter whose calibration sensitivity is not tested, meaning the positive label window boundary is partially determined by retrospective knowledge not isolated from the evaluation corpus.

Heterogeneous graph node attributes for neighboring users may encode post-remediation behavioral signals from confirmed-ATO accounts, constituting label leakage through graph topology even when analyst annotations are excluded.

Class-weighted loss is not an equivalent operation across GNN and GBDT architectures, and differential tuning of this hyperparameter can produce calibration differences that masquerade as representational advantage.

The GNN faces structural inductive failure on unseen nodes in the test period in a way that is asymmetric with GBDT degradation, confounding the comparison of representational advantage with inductive robustness under distribution shift.
all_issues_adjudicated: The backdated proxy label anchor is an implicit free parameter whose calibration sensitivity is not tested, meaning the positive label window boundary is partially determined by retrospective knowledge not isolated from the evaluation corpus.

Heterogeneous graph node attributes for neighboring users may encode post-remediation behavioral signals from confirmed-ATO accounts, constituting label leakage through graph topology even when analyst annotations are excluded.

Class-weighted loss is not an equivalent operation across GNN and GBDT architectures, and differential tuning of this hyperparameter can produce calibration differences that masquerade as representational advantage.

The GNN faces structural inductive failure on unseen nodes in the test period in a way that is asymmetric with GBDT degradation, confounding the comparison of representational advantage with inductive robustness under distribution shift.
